title: Global Life Expectancy Analysis
text:
📥 Load dataset
df_raw <- read_csv(“projects/project1/API_SP.DYN.LE00.IN_DS2_en_csv_v2_121663.csv”, skip = 4) df_raw

title: Global Life Expectancy Analysis
text:
Cleaning Data

df_clean <- df_raw %>%
  select(`Country Name`, starts_with("20")) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -`Country Name`,
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "life_expectancy"
  ) %>%
  mutate(year = as.numeric(year)) %>%
  drop_na()
df_clean

# A tibble: 6,625 × 3
   `Country Name`  year life_expectancy
   <chr>          <dbl>           <dbl>
 1 Aruba           2000            72.9
 2 Aruba           2001            73.0
 3 Aruba           2002            73.1
 4 Aruba           2003            73.2
 5 Aruba           2004            73.2
 6 Aruba           2005            73.4
 7 Aruba           2006            73.5
 8 Aruba           2007            73.6
 9 Aruba           2008            73.8
10 Aruba           2009            74.0
# ℹ 6,615 more rows



##Global Trend Visualization

df_global <- df_clean %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(mean_life = mean(life_expectancy, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(df_global, aes(x = year, y = mean_life)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue", size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Global Life Expectancy Trend",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Life Expectancy"
  )

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.

title: Global Life Expectancy Analysis
text:
Visualization
df_global <- df_clean %>% group_by(year) %>% summarise(mean_life = mean(life_expectancy, na.rm = TRUE))
ggplot(df_global, aes(x = year, y = mean_life)) + geom_line(color = “blue”, size = 1) + labs( title = “Global Life Expectancy Trend”, x = “Year”, y = “Life Expectancy” )

title: Global Life Expectancy Analysis
text:
Insight
Global life expectancy has shown a consistent increasing trend over the last two decades.
For nearly two decades, the line climbs at a remarkably steady and predictable rate. This reflects immense global strides in reducing child mortality, improving access to clean water, expanding vaccine availability, and advancing medical treatments worldwide.
Starting in 2020, the steady upward trend is abruptly broken. Global life expectancy plummets sharply over a two-year period, bottoming out in 2021 at approximately 71.4 years. A drop of more than a full year on a global scale is an extraordinary demographic shock. This visualizes the collective impact of the COVID-19 pandemic—not just from direct virus fatalities, but also from strained healthcare systems worldwide that disrupted treatment for other conditions.
By 2023 and 2024, the line completely recovers from the pandemic slump and surges to its highest point on the graph, reaching nearly 73.5 years. This indicates that once the acute crisis phase of the pandemic passed, global healthcare systems and population health metrics rapidly corrected back to their long-term growth trajectories.

title: Global Life Expectancy Analysis
text:
Countries Comparison
selected_countries <- df_clean %>% filter(Country Name %in% c(“Indonesia”, “Australia”, “Brazil”))
ggplot(selected_countries, aes(year, life_expectancy, color = Country Name)) + geom_line(size = 1) + labs( title = “Life Expectancy Comparison”, y = “Life Expectancy” )
quarto::quarto_render(“projects/project1/analysis.qmd”)

title: Public Health & Data Insights

About Me
I am a medical doctor and public health postgraduate student with a strong interest in epidemiology, biostatistics, and health data analysis.
My work focuses on transforming complex health datasets into meaningful insights that inform policy and intervention strategies.

title: Insights
text:
Explore my thoughts and analyses on global and local public health issues. This space brings together a selection of essays I have written, offering insights into health trends, policy discussions and data-driven perspectives in public health.
Click on the titles below to read the full essays.


A critical analysis of proposed school-based nutrition policies in Australia.



A comparative analysis of health financing in a high-income and middle-income country.

title: Global Life Expectancy Analysis
text:
Insight
The graph reveals three distinct tiers of life expectancy across the entire 24-year period: - High tier (Australia), consistently holds the highest life expectancy, starting near 79 years in 2000 and climbing toward the mid-80s by 2024. - Mid tier (Brazil), sits firmly in the middle, starting just under 70 years and rising to around 76 years. - Lower tier (Indonesia), consistently records the lowest life expectancy of the three, starting around 66 years and ending just above 71 years.
From 2000 up until 2009, all three countries experienced a steady, positive upward trajectory. This might reflect global improvements in healthcare, sanitation, and living standards.
The most striking feature of the graph is the dramatic drop in life expectancy around 2020-2021. This sharp downward spike is highly characteristic of the global impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. Interestingly, Australia’s line does not show a sharp drop during 2020-2021. Instead, it plateaus and slightly rounds off. This reflects Australia’s strict early pandemic border controls and isolation measures, which delayed or minimised initial excess mortality compared to Brazil and Indonesia.
Following the pandemic shock, the graph shows a rapid “V-shaped” recovery for the affected nations (Brazil and Indonesia). Australia shows a slight, minor dip or stabilisation in the final years (2022-2024), which aligns with the period when the country opened its borders.

text:

The graph reveals three distinct tiers of life expectancy across the entire 24-year period: - High tier (Australia), consistently holds the highest life expectancy, starting near 79 years in 2000 and climbing toward the mid-80s by 2024. - Mid tier (Brazil), sits firmly in the middle, starting just under 70 years and rising to around 76 years. - Lower tier (Indonesia), consistently records the lowest life expectancy of the three, starting around 66 years and ending just above 71 years.

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
This essay critically reviews the following article published on The Conversation:
Australian kids BYO lunches to school – There is a healthier way to feed students
Spencer, Kent, & Williams (2025)
🔗 https://theconversation.com/australian-kids-byo-lunches-to-school-there-is-a-healthier-way-to-feed-students-257465

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
This essay critically reviews the following article published on The Conversation:
Australian kids BYO lunches to school – There is a healthier way to feed students
Spencer, Kent, & Williams (2025)
🔗 https://theconversation.com/australian-kids-byo-lunches-to-school-there-is-a-healthier-way-to-feed-students-257465

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
Introduction
Australia has many working parents struggling to prepare nutrient-dense school lunches for their children. According to the Australian Bureau of Statistics (2025a), about 73% of couples raising children aged 0-14 are dual-earner families with limited time to perform meal preparation. In such a situation, Spencer, Kent, and Williams (2025) reported that more than 80% of Australian primary school lunchboxes fell short of nutritional standards, and that junk food accounted for half of students’ intake. As a consequence of low-quality lunchboxes, children may experience significant long-term health impacts, including increased risk of obesity and chronic diseases (Gallegos et al., 2025).
Therefore, Spencer, Kent, and Williams (2025) proposed a universal school meal program as a population-level, prevention-focused approach to address the poor nutrition issue among Australian students.

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
Overview of the Proposed Policy
Spencer, Kent, and Williams (2025) propose a universal school meal program as a population-level intervention to address poor dietary quality among Australian schoolchildren.
The policy aims to provide nutritious meals to all students regardless of socioeconomic background, with particular benefits for vulnerable populations, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children.

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
Strengths of the Policy Proposal
Finland and Sweden are early adopters of the universal school meal program and are considered highly successful pilot models (Spencer et al., 2025). The implementation of universal school meal programs over the past seven decades in Finland and Sweden has led to improvements in the health, social, environmental, and community sectors (Gallegos et al., 2025). For instance, it has been reported that the programs have significantly changed the dietary habits of schoolchildren in both countries, shifting from high consumption of processed foods to a vitamin-rich diet.
Reflecting on these success stories, Gallegos (2025) also highlighted that the universal school meal program should be implemented more systematically, not exclusively, to address nutritional issues among schoolchildren. A more integrated approach may influence children’s health by strengthening social and cultural well-being, improving food and nutrition security, supporting local food systems and advancing toward sustainable food systems (Gallegos et al., 2025). This evidence shows that the Australian government could adopt these international models to invest in extensive and equitable school meal programs.

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
Implementation Challenges

Geographic and Logistical Barriers
Despite the potential merits, the Australian government needs to consider the challenges of implementing the universal school meal program. Firstly, compared with Finland and Sweden, Australia is twenty times larger, with an area of nearly 7.7 million km2 (Australian Bureau of Statistics [ABS], 2012, para. 1). These geographic barriers pose immense operational challenges in the food supply chain.
This issue can be illustrated by analysing Indonesia, a neighbouring country whose vast area is comparable to that of Australia. In January 2025, the Indonesian government launched its universal school meal program to address the triple burden of malnutrition, in which undernutrition, overnutrition, and micronutrient deficiencies simultaneously occur (Andriani et al., 2023; Surendra et al., 2026). Beyond its aspirational purpose, the program has faced major operational weaknesses due to the hastily arranged implementation. Aiming to maintain national quality consistency and suppress national funds, the Indonesian government uses a centralized logistics system while overlooking the country’s archipelagic structure and size (Surendra et al., 2026). These decisions appear to have contributed to regional disparities, logistical delays, and persistent outbreaks of food poisoning.
A recent annual evaluation of this program reported that food poisoning cases account for over 20,000 students in nearly 25% of districts in Indonesia (Surendra et al., 2026).
This is a concrete example of the lack of integrated standards and multisectoral collaboration across the country in policy execution, which has led to catastrophic events.


Cultural Diversity and Health Equity
Another challenge worth highlighting is Australia’s demographic diversity. Australia is home to 276 unique cultures and ethnic groups (ABS, 2025b, para. 25). Therefore, ensuring national nutrition guidelines that promote cultural diversity and inclusivity is important, as some cultures have dietary restrictions rooted in their beliefs. Essential measures involve local procurement, culturally appropriate menus, regional preparation centres and strengthened food safety protocols. More importantly, the participation of the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities in proposing a new policy is compulsory.
Cartwright (2025) reported that previous policies created to improve health equity between Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander People and non-Indigenous Australians did not result as expected due to the absence of First Nations voices in policy-making. These failures arise from insufficient insider knowledge, including a lack of genuine understanding of community priorities and limited engagement with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander ontological and epistemological frameworks (Cartwright et al., 2025).
In the context of the food landscape, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities have practiced a food system that combines complex scientific observation with land care practices rooted in reciprocity and community values. Colonialism has significantly erased this ancestral system, which has evidently contributed to the rising prevalence of non-communicable diseases due to the shift toward Western food consumption (Cartwright et al., 2025). Considering these issues, prioritizing cultural determinants of health and integrating them with social determinants is critical for developing effective strategies to address health disparities (Cartwright et al., 2025).

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
Critical Reflection
Finally, while I agree that the universal school meal program may be a viable alternative to address poor nutrition, food insecurity, and socioeconomic disparities, I believe a more careful approach to its implementation is needed. The success of this program depends on addressing structural and cultural barriers discussed beforehand. Consequently, considerable funding and ongoing government investment are required to achieve the program’s aim and tackle the challenges it faces (Cartwright et al., 2025).
Intersectoral collaboration among governments, subject-matter experts such as public health nutritionists, community representatives, school administrators and educators, parents and guardians is mandatory to incorporate insights and feedback (Gallegos et al., 2025). Ongoing monitoring and reporting are also required to maintain the quality of the program’s long-term evaluation. Existing evidence suggests that the universal school meal program could significantly improve children’s health, but only if implemented through a culturally sensitive, operational excellence and adequately funded public health strategy.

title: Critique of Universal School Meal Policy in Australia
text:
References
Article Reference

Spencer, L., Kent, K., & Williams, M. (2025, June 4). Australian kids BYO lunches to school. There is a healthier way to feed students. The Conversation. https://theconversation.com/australian-kids-byo-lunches-to-school-there-is-a-healthier-way-to-feed-students-257465

Primary References

Cartwright, J., Turner, N., Bruce, S., Sultanbawa, Y. F., Netzel, M. E., & Wright, O. R. L. (2025). Exploring the perspectives of urban and regional living Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples regarding bush foods, nutrition and health: insights for culturally informed health policy in Australia. Public Health Nutr, 28(1), e124. https://doi.org/10.1017/S1368980025100694
Gallegos, D., Manson, A., Vidgen, H. A., Byrne, R., & Johnson, B. J. (2025). School-Provided Meals and the Prevention of Childhood Obesity: A Small part of a Very Important Story. Curr Obes Rep, 14(1), 44. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13679-025-00635-x
Surendra, H., Sujagat, A., & Wangge, G. (2026). Mitigating food safety risks in Indonesia’s free school meals programme. Lancet Reg Health Southeast Asia, 46, 100734. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lansea.2026.100734

Additional References

Andriani, H., Friska, E., Arsyi, M., Sutrisno, A. E., Waits, A., & Rahmawati, N. D. (2023). A multilevel analysis of the triple burden of malnutrition in Indonesia: trends and determinants from repeated cross-sectional surveys. BMC Public Health, 23(1), 1836. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12889-023-16728-y
Australian Bureau of Statistics. (2012). Geography of Australia. https://www.abs.gov.au/ausstats/abs@.nsf/Lookup/by%20Subject/1301.02012Main%20FeaturesGeography%20of%20Australia12
Australian Bureau of Statistics. (2025). Australian Standard Classification of Cultural and Ethnic Groups (ASCCEG): Latest release. Australian Bureau of Statistics. https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/standards/australian-statistical-geography-standard-asgs-edition-3/latest-release
Australian Bureau of Statistics. (2025). Labour force status of families. https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/labour/employment-and-unemployment/labour-force-status-families/latest-release

title: Insights
text: A critical analysis of proposed school-based nutrition policies in Australia.

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Health financing systems play a critical role in protecting individuals from financial risk, particularly where out-of-pocket payments remain a major source of funding. Sustainable health financing requires diversified and pooled funding that allocates costs according to ability to pay rather than need [1].
This essay compares the health financing systems of two countries, Australia and Thailand. The comparison focuses on differences in major health financing sources, including their historical and political contexts, key reforms over the past two decades and approaches to financial protection for the poor. The comparison results are summarised in Table 1 and the following sections of this essay will elaborate on these results further.

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Health financing systems play a critical role in protecting individuals from financial risk, particularly where out-of-pocket payments remain a major source of funding. Sustainable health financing requires diversified and pooled funding that allocates costs according to ability to pay rather than need [1].
This essay compares the health financing systems of two countries, Australia and Thailand. The comparison focuses on differences in major health financing sources, including their historical and political contexts, key reforms over the past two decades and approaches to financial protection for the poor. The comparison results are summarised in Table 1 and the following sections of this essay will elaborate on these results further.

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Primary Sources of Health Financing
Australia and Thailand’s health financing arrangements differ substantially in funding sources. According to the Health Expenditure Australia 2023-24 data, Australia’s health financing system is predominantly tax-funded, with a significant role for private health insurance and out-of-pocket (OOP) payments, reflecting its mixed public-private model (Table 1) [2] Meanwhile, Thailand’s health financing system relies on a more centralised public financing model, in which almost 70% of the Universal Coverage Scheme (UCS) is funded by general government revenues [3]. The system is organised around three public insurance schemes, with the tax-financed UCS serving as the dominant platform for coverage and financial protection [3, 4].




Table 1: Comparison of Health Financing Structures in Australia and Thailand

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Historical and Political Context
The contrasting financing models of Australia and Thailand reflect their distinct historical and political trajectories. Australia’s approach to Universal Health Coverage (UHC) has developed gradually through political compromise. Medicare, introduced in the 1980s, is a tax-financed framework that guarantees access to public hospital services and subsidised medical care [5]. At the same time, governments chose not to dismiss the existing private insurance sector. Instead, Commonwealth subsidies and tax incentives were maintained to support private insurance, embedding a mixed public-private financing model [6]. Australia’s federal structure has further shaped this arrangement, with funding and service responsibilities split between the Commonwealth and the states, particularly for hospitals [5].
On the other hand, Thailand’s experience with UHC is characterised by rapid change driven by political momentum and explicit equity goals. Before the early 2000s, health coverage in Thailand was fragmented, with multiple insurance schemes that left large segments of the population, especially informal sector workers, without adequate protection [7]. The introduction of the UCS in 2002 was built on years of policy development by public health experts and was propelled by electoral competition that elevated universal health care to a national priority [8]. Policymakers adopted a tax financed model that redefined access to health care as an entitlement. This approach was closely tied to cost-containment measures, reflecting the fiscal constraints of a middle-income country [8].

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Major Health Financing Reforms
Over the past two decades, Australia and Thailand have implemented significant health financing reforms. In Australia, reforms have focused on improving efficiency and accountability within an established universal system [2]. The National Health Reform Agreement (NHRA) and the introduction of the ABF in 2011 aimed to improve efficiency, interstate equity and transparency in health care expenditures [2, 9, 10]. Further changes under the Federation Funding Agreement after 2020 sought to streamline fragmented Commonwealth–state funding arrangements and clarify roles in hospital and preventive service financing [2]. Moreover, recent Strengthening Medicare reforms (2022–2025) have targeted primary care affordability, workforce shortages and system integrity [11, 12].
On the other hand, Thailand was rapidly expanding UHC in the early 2000s. The tax financed model prioritised cost containment through capitation, global budgets and strong primary care gatekeeping, allowing rapid coverage expansion while maintaining fiscal control [13]. Framed as an equity driven nation building project, the UCS explicitly sought to reduce disparities in access and financial risk that had left around 30% of the population uninsured prior to 2001 [3]. More recent reforms in 2020 under the SAFE (Sustainable, Affordable, Fair and Efficient) framework addressed rising costs, population aging and benefit disparities across insurance schemes [14].
The differing nature of these reforms can be explained by each country’s institutional and political context. Australia already has universal coverage established, alongside a strong private insurance sector and a federal system that promoted incremental reform [2]. Consequently, reforms focus on system refinement and workforce sustainability rather than redistribution [2]. Thailand, by contrast, pursued UHC as a nation building project at a moment of political opportunity, enabling more decisive reforms centered on equity and financial risk protection [8].

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Financial Protection for Vulnerable Populations
As Australia and Thailand commit to UHC, the degree of financial protection afforded to poor households differs substantially between the two systems. In Australia, the health financing system suggests a strong foundation for protecting households from excessive health care costs. Medicare provides universal, tax financed access to public hospital care free at the point of use, while also subsidising medical services and medicines through the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme (PBS) [12]. In addition, the Australian government’s subsidies for private health insurance aim to increase consumer choice and reduce demand for public hospitals. However, research suggests that these subsidies have only a limited effect on overall system capacity [15, 16]. Evidence points to persistent affordability challenges in primary care. Declining bulk-billing rates and fee-for-service pricing have increased OOP costs, yet gaps in routinely reported financial risk indicators limit the assessment of Medicare’s equity performance [17-20].
Thailand’s financial protection outcomes are more clearly documented. Only about 2% of Thai households spend more than 10% of their total consumption on health care, which is among the lowest rates of catastrophic health expenditure in the region [3, 21]. This reflects near-universal enrolment across public schemes (Table 1) [21]. Key structural features include minimal or zero user fees, capitation payments for outpatient care, DRG with global budgets for inpatient services and a strong primary care gatekeeping system that reduces reliance on costly hospital care [8].
As summarised in Table 1, Thailand offers stronger financial protection for the poor by combining explicit equity objectives, comprehensive tax financing and strict cost control mechanisms. Meanwhile, Australia lacks recently published financial risk indicators, which prevents direct comparison. These differences reflect broader structural and policy choices: Thailand’s reforms prioritised reducing financial hardship and expanding equity, while Australia has focused on system efficiency, digital modernisation and workforce capacity rather than directly lowering OOP costs.

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Policy Recommendations and Challenges
Evidence indicates that financial protection for low income populations can be strengthened through targeted health financing reforms. Australia’s recent investments under the Strengthening Medicare agenda reflect government recognition of long term underinvestment in primary care and persistent workforce shortages. Rising OOP costs and workforce gaps stem from interconnected structural pressures, including growing demand for care, insufficient remuneration in general practice, workforce maldistribution, and the flexibility of fee for service pricing under Medicare [5, 22].
Expanding bulk billing incentives in primary care therefore, offers clear equity benefits. Reducing gap fees lessens the immediate financial burden on disadvantaged households, who are more likely to delay care due to cost [19]. Improved affordability of first contact care can also reduce avoidable hospital admissions by supporting earlier management of chronic conditions and, over time, lowering health inequalities and subsequent health system costs [20, 22, 23]. Nonetheless, implementation challenges remain. Workforce shortages—particularly in rural and remote areas—may limit practices’ capacity to meet increased demand, while inadequate indexation of incentives may continue to deter bulk billing [12, 16, 18].
In Thailand, strengthening financial protection requires harmonising benefit packages across the public insurance schemes. Despite strong overall financial protection, disparities in entitlements generate inequities in access to and quality of care, particularly for UCS beneficiaries, who are predominantly low-income or informal-sector workers [8, 21]. Harmonisation would enhance equity by aligning access to essential and high cost services with health needs rather than employment status, limiting incentives for cost shifting. Benefit and payment alignment could improve efficiency and reduce fragmentation [13, 24]. However, progress is constrained by political resistance from CSMBS beneficiaries, who receive more generous service entitlements, as well as the rising fiscal pressures linked to population aging [3, 8, 25].

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand
text:
Conclusion
Overall, the comparison between Australia and Thailand illustrates how differences in health financing design can lead to markedly different outcomes in equity and financial protection. The UHC alone does not guarantee equity or effective financial protection. Thailand’s tax financed UCS, supported by strong cost control mechanisms, has delivered clear protection against financial hardship for low income and informal sector populations [8]. Australia’s mixed financing model ensures broad access in principle but continues to face affordability, workforce, and coordination challenges. Aligning health financing reforms more explicitly with equity objectives remains essential to ensuring that universal coverage translates into meaningful, affordable access to care for those most at risk.

title: Two Systems, Two Strategies: Health Financing and Universal Coverage in Australia and Thailand

title: Insights
text: A comparative analysis of health financing in a high-income and middle-income country.
